g.text: Scenario D - Repeated Miswire with Teacher Handoff
Escalation after multiple unsuccessful attempts and declining student confidence.
Chen Yinuo - Introductory DC Lab - Objective: Explain why a voltmeter should be
connected across a target component rather than inserted into the main loop.
The repeated measurement miswire indicates that the learner now needs live teacher
mediation rather than another round of autonomous prompting.
Escalation is forced by repeated unsuccessful attempts in the showcase metadata.
Place the voltmeter correctly while preserving a meaningful
Explain why a voltmeter should be connected across a target
component rather than inserted into the main loop.
OPEN_CIRCUIT - The source does not see a valid conductive return path.
VOLTMETER_SERIES - The voltmeter has been inserted into the main loop.
- The voltmeter is sitting inside the main loop instead of across the target element.
- No closed return path exists between the source terminals.
- Reconnect the voltmeter across the load terminals.
- Restore a complete conductive loop before interpreting any readings.
- Escalate to instructor-led correction after repeated or safety-sensitive errors.
- Use the high-impedance model to explain why the loop current collapses.
The measurement chain behaves like an artificial open path because the voltmeter has been
inserted into the main loop instead of across the target load.
The learner has attached a high-impedance instrument as though it were part of the
conductive path. This creates a strong opportunity to explain why voltmeters reveal
potential difference rather than carry the main loop current.
- Chen Yinuo is currently working on 'Place the voltmeter correctly while preserving a
meaningful current path.' in scenario 'Scenario D - Repeated Miswire with Teacher Handoff'.
- The system classifies the circuit state as Instrument Miswire with risk level elevated
- Pause autonomous guidance, bring the instructor into the loop, and use the current state
This scenario is ideal for demonstrating the handoff boundary between automated scaffolding
GMAT showcase mode: from circuit state reasoning to instructional action.
